items.details: Most assistance programs require a score of 620 or higher. A higher score often leads to better interest rates.
Aim for at least 1% to 3% of the home price, even if you plan to use a grant. Lenders like to see that you have your own 'skin in the game'.
Lenders look at your Debt-to-Income (DTI) ratio. Ideally, your total monthly debt payments should be less than 43% of your gross monthly income.
Gather your signed Federal tax returns for the last two years, including all schedules.
Collect your W-2 forms from all employers for the last two years.
Save your most recent consecutive pay stubs covering at least the last 30 days.
Provide the last two months of statements for all your bank and investment accounts (all pages included).
Ensure you have a valid, unexpired government-issued ID, such as a Driver's License or Passport.
Mandatory for SONYMA and most grant programs. You must use a HUD-approved housing counseling agency.

glossary.definition: An extra monthly fee you pay if your down payment is less than 20%. It protects the lender if you stop making payments, but it adds to your monthly cost.
A neutral account where the lender holds a portion of your monthly payment to pay for your property taxes and home insurance when they are due.
The percentage of your monthly income (before taxes) that goes toward paying debts like credit cards, student loans, and your future mortgage.
A loan where the interest rate never changes. Your monthly payment for the loan itself stays the same for 15 or 30 years.
A loan where the interest rate can go up or down over time. While it may start lower than a fixed rate, your monthly payment can increase significantly later.
One-time fees paid at the very end of the home buying process. These include things like lawyer fees, taxes, and title insurance, usually totaling 2% to 5% of the home's price.
